coredata.dc:title: pH-sensitive green tea-derived carbon quantum dots for real-time monitoring of shrimp freshness
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Khan, Ajahar
Ezati, Parya
Rhim, Jong-Whan
Kim, Jun Tae
Molaei, Rahim
coredata.dc:description: Green-emitting carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal approach using green tea leaf powder (GLP) as a single precursor. The stability and effectiveness of the synthesized GLP-CDs were verified by zeta potential, optical, and luminescence analyses. The prepared CD has excellent pH-responsive properties, providing exciting possibilities for pH-induced intelligent monitoring of packaged foods. GLP-CD was useful for determining liquid ammonia in the linear range of 1–100 μM with the lowest detection limit of 0.4 μM. Additionally, the paper indicator coated with GLP-CDs exhibits a sharp color change from yellow to brown, indicating its ability to detect ammonia vapor. The efficacy of the GLP-CDs-based paper indicator was evaluated as a real-time freshness indicator of shrimp packaging by determining total volatile basic nitrogen. The paper indicator based on GLP-CD has proven to be a simple and cost-effective system for visually monitoring shrimp freshness in the field for suppliers and consumers.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
PH-responsive
Colorimetric detection
Freshness indicator

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Application of GLP-CDs indicator for shrimp packaging
Sensitivity to acid and ammonia vapors
Application of GLP-CDs indicator for shrimp packaging

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean government (MSIT) ( 2022R1A2B5B02001422 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of (a) GLP-CDs preparation and (b) application of GLP-CDs indicator in shrimp packaging.

(a) TEM image of the GLP-CDs and (b) size histogram of the GLP-CDs with a curve fitted to the data using a Gaussian model, (c) FTIR spectrum, (d) XPS full survey scan, and corresponding high-resolution scans of (e) C 1 s, (f) N 1 s, and (g) O 1 s.

(a) Photographs of GLP-CDs exposed to different buffer solutions under daylight and UV light, (b) fluorescence emission spectra of GLP-CDs were recorded at different pH, (c) illustration of responsive mechanisms of GLP-CDs towards pH, (d) fluorescence spectra of GLP-CDs at various concentrations of NH3, and (e) plot of the quenching intensity versus NH3 concentration.
(a) Photographs and (b) color parameters of GLP-CDs indicator exposed to different buffer solutions, and (c) the sensitivity of the proposed indicator toward ammonia and acid vapors.
Shrimp freshness monitoring by GLP-CDs indicator under daylight and UV light.

head.ce:title: pH-sensitive green tea-derived carbon quantum dots for real-time monitoring of shrimp freshness

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: BioNanocomposite Research Center, Department of Food and Nutrition, Kyung Hee University, 26 Kyungheedae-ro, Dongdaemun-gu, Seoul 02447, the Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: BioNanocomposite Research Center, Department of Food and Nutrition, Kyung Hee University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: BioNanocomposite Research Center, Department of Food and Nutrition, Kyung Hee University, 26 Kyungheedae-ro, Dongdaemun-gu, Seoul 02447, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food Hygiene and Quality Control, Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, Urmia University, Urmia, the Islamic Republic of Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Food Hygiene and Quality Control, Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, Urmia University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Food Hygiene and Quality Control, Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, Urmia University, Urmia, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Green-emitting carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal approach using green tea leaf powder (GLP) as a single precursor. The stability and effectiveness of the synthesized GLP-CDs were verified by zeta potential, optical, and luminescence analyses. The prepared CD has excellent pH-responsive properties, providing exciting possibilities for pH-induced intelligent monitoring of packaged foods. GLP-CD was useful for determining liquid ammonia in the linear range of 1–100 μM with the lowest detection limit of 0.4 μM. Additionally, the paper indicator coated with GLP-CDs exhibits a sharp color change from yellow to brown, indicating its ability to detect ammonia vapor. The efficacy of the GLP-CDs-based paper indicator was evaluated as a real-time freshness indicator of shrimp packaging by determining total volatile basic nitrogen. The paper indicator based on GLP-CD has proven to be a simple and cost-effective system for visually monitoring shrimp freshness in the field for suppliers and consumers.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Maintaining quality and securing safety to extend the shelf life of food is one of the most important issues in the food market. Inadequate control and monitoring at different stages of the food supply chain (i.e., production, processing, storage, marketing, handling, distribution, and consumption) results in food waste or loss [1]. A rapidly growing population is estimated to increase food demand by 150–170 % of current levels by 2050 [2]. Population growth and the demand for protein-rich and nutritious food sources will lead to increased consumption of animal food products such as meat and seafood. However, seafood and meat products are composed of protein, high fat, and water, providing a favorable medium for microbial growth and rapid spoilage [3]. Shrimp are more susceptible to spoilage even under cold storage conditions due to the effective psychrophilic spoilage microorganisms [4]. These putrefactive microorganisms break down the macromolecules present in food into various low molecular weight compounds. The various metabolites produced during meat decay are nitrogen-containing molecules such as alcohols, aldehydes, hydrogen sulfide, acetic and lactic acid, ammonia, and amines. Therefore, it is essential to design reliable, effective, and convenient methods to determine the quality of packaged food from a food safety and human health perspective [3].

Advanced active and intelligent packaging systems are the most capable pathways to maintaining food quality and safety and have recently received more attention due to the prospect of implementing detectors, sensors, antioxidants, antimicrobials, oxygen/ethylene scavengers, and preservatives to slow spoilage [5,6]. Active packaging can provide effective shelf life extension in the food supply and does not convey dynamic information about food freshness and quality to producers, retailers, or consumers in real-time [7]. Therefore, developing a practical, dynamic, and reliable system for quality tracking during food supply is necessary.

The main spoilage indicators of protein-rich seafood and meat products are evidenced by biogenic amines, highly correlated with meat quality indices, for example, total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N) concentration and pH [8]. TVB-N is an amine-based substance, including NH3 (ammonia), dimethylamine, and trimethylamine, which are products of amino acid metabolism. Determining desired analytes to ensure food safety requires various sophisticated analytical instruments such as nuclear magnetic resonance, mass spectrometry, ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) spectroscopy, chromatography, and flow injection analysis [9]. Most of these techniques have been difficult to use everywhere in food samples due to the drawbacks of laborious and time-consuming sample preparation, expensive equipment, and skilled operators [10]. Thus, scientists have been focusing on constructing more promising, scalable, and non-destructive indicators such as near-infrared spectroscopy-based sensors, radio-frequency identification (RFID) tags, and optical and colorimetric sensors [11]. Of these convenient indicators, the composition of the pH-sensitive indicator was more meaningful due to its noticeable color change and was highly responsive, fast, compact, and instrument-free. Additionally, nearly all freshness indicators used pH-responsive synthetic dyes that produced visual, clear, and generic indications [12]. However, synthetic dyes (bromocresol purple, bromothymol blue, bromocresol green, methyl red, cresol red, xylenol, chlorophenol, etc.) are not suitable for human consumption because they have toxic and carcinogenic effects on human health [12–14].

Compared to other materials, paper-based indicators are cost-effective, eco-friendly, renewable, biocompatible, biodegradable, widely available, multi-fiber porous structure, and provide strong support for functional coatings. The paper has been widely used to make freshness indicators containing pH-sensitive synthetic dyes and volatile organic compounds incorporated into filter paper. In the past few years, the development of environmentally friendly and safe intelligent indicators based on natural resources to replace synthetic chemical dyes has increased [15].

Carbon dots (CDs) are a new class of carbon-based nanomaterials that are of great interest due to their excellent chemical properties and photostability, abundant functional groups, low toxicity, biocompatibility, and high tunable fluorescence [16–19]. CD applications in fields such as biological imaging, photovoltaic devices, solar cells, and drug delivery have been rapidly increasing [20]. Especially for the prospects of pH indicators, CDs look promising due to their ease of synthesis, high sensitivity, fast reaction, and easy detection [21]. The pH response phenomenon is evaluated due to the protonation and deprotonation of anti-counterfeiting functional groups on the CD's surface [22]. The photoluminescence mechanism of CDs is still controversial, even for pH-sensitive CDs, because of the complex and uncertain morphology of these nanomaterials [23]. According to previous reports, CD was used as a potential crosslinker and activator to fabricate colorant doping indicator systems for food freshness monitoring [18,24]. Most investigations have shown that colorants mixed with CD respond to color changes and quench fluorescence with pH changes [25]. For example, colorimetric gelatin films based on red cabbage anthocyanin extract and CD were fabricated to monitor food spoilage based on color change. The color change of colorimetric gelatin films at different pH was mainly due to anthocyanins [26]. In another report, a biocompatibility indicator for food freshness was prepared using citrus peel-derived CD integrated with resazurin. In this investigation, resazurin was the main substance responsible for the color change of the CD/resazurin indicator at different pHs [27].

Green tea is well known for its abundance of active ingredients such as catechins (mainly epicatequinagalatto, epigallocatechin, and epicatechin), caffeine, tea polyphenols, amino acids, vitamins, chlorophyll, and more. These substances have been investigated for their anti-inflammatory, anti-oxidation, and anticancer effects [28]. In addition, green tea's active ingredients have a nitrogen-containing structure or polyhydroxy/polycarboxylate structure that can promote the synthesis of CDs [29]. Besides, green tea's effective free radical scavenging activity makes it used as raw materials for medicines and cosmetics [30], which suggests that green tea could be a good source of carbon precursor for synthesizing multifunctional CDs [31–33].

This work aimed to develop a cost-effective, eco-friendly pH-sensitive indicator using filter paper incorporated with green tea leaf powder (GLP)-derived CDs (GLP-CDs) for real-time monitoring of freshness and spoilage detection of shrimp. The indicator was prepared by drop-coating of GLP-CDs solution onto filter paper. The main feature of the proposed GLP-CD-based paper indicator is its relatively simple operation principle, which does not require sophisticated equipment to pass the data to a computer system, such as a radio frequency identification (RFID) system, to obtain logical data on food status [11]. The fabricated CDs-based paper indicator (GLP-CDs indicator) was attached to the inside of the shrimp package as a visual spoilage indicator that responds to pH changes caused by TVB-N occurring in the shrimp during storage.

A one-step hydrothermal method was used to synthesize GLP-CDs, following the procedure shown in Fig. 1a [34]. In brief, 1 g GLP was dispersed in distilled water (50 mL) under mild agitation at room temperature (23 ± 2 °C), transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel reactor (100 mL), and sealed tightly. The reactor was placed in a muffle furnace at 180 °C for 8 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution mixture was filtered through a 100 µm Whatman filter and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 20 min. The supernatant was filtered using a 0.22 µm Whatman filter membrane, and the obtained GLP-CDs solution was stored at 4 °C for further characterization. The concentration of the synthesized GLP-CDs was 5.9 mg/mL. Detailed methods for physicochemical characterization and free radical scavenging activity of synthesized GLP-CDs are presented in the Supporting Information.

The prepared GLP-CDs solution was applied to Whatman No. 42 filter paper by drop coating deposition to prepare a paper indicator. Briefly, indicator carriers were cut into square-sized tags (3.0 × 3.0 cm2), and 2 mL indicator solution (GLP-CDs, 6.1 mg/mL in distilled water) was made into culture tubes. Then, 1.0 mL indicator solution was added dropwise to the indicator carrier and dried at room temperature. After drying the coated indicator carrier, 1.0 mL of the indicator solution was applied and left to dry overnight at room temperature. After that, the paper indicator was ready to use as an indicator for the shrimp packaging test. The methods describing the color parameters, sensitivity toward ammonia and acid vapors, color stability, and statistical analysis of engineered GLP-CDs indicators are presented in the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of GLP-CDs indicator for shrimp packaging
ce:section.ce:para: After adding 100 g of raw shrimp in a UV sterilized glass container (100 cm × 100 cm × 5.5 cm), a GLP-CDs indicator was attached to the inside of the lid at about 2.5 cm above the samples (Fig. 1b), and the lid of the container was sealed. The color change of the GLP-CDs indicator was observed when the packaged sample was stored at 28 °C for 24 h. Three replicates were performed to monitor the color change response of the attached GLP-CDs indicator.

A steam distillation method was used to evaluate the TVB-N concentrations [35]. TVB-N was extracted using a Kjeldahl distillation unit, collected in an Erlenmeyer flask, and titrated with sulfuric acid (0.1 N) to determine the TVB-N evolved. TVB-N concentration values are expressed in mg/100 g sample (mg%). To determine the pH of the shrimp sample, 10 g of the sample was homogenized with 90 mL of distilled water, and the pH of the resulting slurry was measured using a digital pH meter (Thermo Scientific, Indonesia).

The chemical structure and composition of the synthesized GLP-CDs were characterized by FTIR, XPS, FE-SEM, TEM, and zeta potential analyses. The GLP-CDs were synthesized at high temperatures via active substances present in GLP with a polycarboxylate/polyhydroxy structure or a nitrogen-comprising compound serving as the main carbon source [29,36].

The morphology and particle size of the GLP-CDs were determined by TEM. Fig. 2a shows the high-resolution TEM image of the synthesized GLP-CDs. The TEM analysis confirms that the CDs were highly size, spherical, and shape monodisperse with a size distribution (below 10 nm) in the quantum range [5]. The particle diameter for the fabricated GLP-CDs ranges from ∼1.98–5.5 nm. Fig. 2b exhibits a histogram of the CD's diameters, evaluated by analysis of TEM images of GLP-CDs located at different positions. The synthesized CD's mean diameter was ∼4.2 ± 0.825 nm, based on fitting the histogram to a Gaussian model. In addition, the surface zeta potential illustrated that a high negative surface charge of around − 13.4 mV was calculated for GLP-CDs (Table S1, Supporting Information), validating stable aqueous dispersion of the GLP-CDs [37]. According to the previous reports, the values close to or higher than ± 15 mV suggest good stability of the CDs dispersions in an aqueous medium. However, a lower value of zeta potential indicates that attraction surpasses repulsion and tends to aggregate because of the breakdown of dispersion [38]. The EDX and elemental mapping analyses of the GLP-CDs were performed to evaluate the presence of characteristic elements constituting the CDs, and the results are displayed in Fig. S1 (Supporting Information). The EDX spectrum of the GLP-CDs displays the characteristic peaks of main constitutional elements, while the percentages of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen on the surface of the GLP-CDs are shown in the inset image present in the EDX spectrum. The obtained mapping images suggested that the nitrogen and oxygen were unvaryingly dispersed throughout the surface of GLP-CDs. The strong elemental peaks of nitrogen and oxygen, along with carbon, are due to the composition of different oxygenated and nitrogen-containing functional groups on GLP-CDs.

The FTIR spectrum for GLP-CDs is shown in Fig. 2c, the peak positioned around ∼3150–3500 cm−1 assigned to the existence of -OH and N-H bonds stretching. The transmittance peaks observed at 2930 and 1237 cm−1 displayed asymmetric and symmetric stretching of C-H bonds in methylene and methyl groups. Further, the peaks around 1670 cm−1 indicated the CO stretching vibration of ketones and carboxylic groups; 1590 cm−1 attributed to CC and vibration of N-H (amide bending); the peaks at 1120 cm−1 ascribed to C-O-H; the peak at 1014 cm−1 to C-O groups in polysaccharides and the peak around 1407 cm−1 was indicated to the stretching vibration modes C-N. For the obtained FTIR result, some of the peaks may be overlapped due to the similar -OH, C-H, CC, and N-H stretching vibration of different functions in the same region.

The XPS analysis was performed to probe the chemical composition and existence of the constitutional elements of as-prepared GLP-CDs. Fig. 2d shows the full-survey spectrum of GLP-CDs. The 284.52, 398.97, and 530.69 eV peaks indicate carbon (C 1 s of sp2C), N 1 s, and O 1 s of the surface functional groups, respectively [39]. Additionally, the survey scan suggested that the surface of CDs comprises 68.56 at% of C 1 s, 10.18 at% of O 1 s, and 21.16 at% of N 1 s at the corresponding binding energies (Table S2, Supporting Information); the results obtained by the XPS matched well with EDX shown in Fig. S1 (Supporting Information). The high-resolution XPS C1s spectrum of GLP-CDs (Fig. 2e) can be deconvoluted into four component peaks located at ∼283.98 (reflects the bonding structure of C-H or C-C bonds), ∼284.99, and ∼285.78 (attributed to the C-N and C-O bonds) and ∼287.18 eV (corresponds to the O-C-O or CO of carbonyl) [39]. As shown in Fig. 2f, the deconvoluted XPS N1s spectra resolved into the fitting of three component peaks located around 398.5, 399.2, and 400.0 eV attributed due to the C-N-C, tertiary N bonded to carbon atoms in the form of N-(C)3, and N-H bonds, respectively [39,40]. Further, the high-resolution XPS O 1 s spectrum deconvoluted into three peaks of ∼530.2 eV, ∼531.8 eV, and ∼532.4 eV is attributed to C-O/N-O, CO, and C-O-H groups, respectively (Fig. 2g) [39,41].

The optical properties of the CDs were analyzed using UV–visible light absorption spectroscopy. In Fig. 3a, the UV-Vis absorption spectrum in the range of 200–700 exhibits a similar absorption peak consistent with previously reported CDs synthesized by Roy and Rhim [5]. The aqueous solution of the CDs emitted light green under full band UV irradiation due to the quantum confinement phenomenon (right inset, Fig. 3a). Instantaneous color change under UV irradiation due to the quantum confinement effect is a characteristic phenomenon of CD, and the intensity of color change is determined by the carbon source and preparation route adopted for CD synthesis [42]. In Fig. 3a, the UV-Vis absorption spectrum of GLP-CDs displays typical absorption peaks at 296 nm ascribed to n-π * transitions (CO or C-OH bonds) and a shouldering peak at ∼282 nm assigned to π-π * transitions (C-C bonds with sp2 hybridization) [42].

Further, fluorescence spectroscopy was used to investigate the fluorescent characteristics of the CDs. Fig. 3b shows the prepared CDs' excitation-dependent fluorescent emission (300–430 nm). The spectra show a typical excitation wavelength-dependent response, and under excitation, with longer wavelengths, the emission wavelength is red-shifted [43]. Therefore, the increased excitation wavelength from 300 to 430 nm demonstrated a clear, steady bathochromic shift of emission peaks. When the excitation wavelength was 360 nm, the highest fluorescent emission intensity was observed, around ∼440 nm (Fig. 3a). In addition, since the emission effect varies depending on the particle size distribution of the CD, the particle size, and the emission site on the surface, the emission wavelength can be adjusted [5,44,45]. However, the exact fluorescent mechanism accounting for the excitation wavelength-dependent emission is still complicated and needs to be explored. In addition, the quantum yield of GLP-CD was calculated to be 11.7 %, which was sufficient to develop a fluorescence-responsive food freshness indication system.

Photographs of the GLP-CDs in different pH solutions at room temperature were taken in daylight and UV light, as shown in Fig. 4a. Under acidic conditions (pH = 4–6), the GLP-CDs solution had no color change. At pH 7, the color turned brownish, and as the pH rose to pH 10, the hue became more intense. Importantly, the GLP-CDs solution showed mild UV fluorescence; at pH 7, a green hue developed, and when the pH was raised to more alkaline conditions, the color intensified. The shift in GLP's chemical structure causes color variations when the pH is more basic.

To understand the pH-responsive mechanism of the CDs, the fluorescent intensity (at maximum excitation wavelength; 360 nm) of the GLP-CDs was determined at different pH ranges (4−10). As depicted in Fig. 4b, the fluorescence intensity of GLP-CDs has been influenced by different pH, and its fluorescence intensity is pH-dependent. This effect of pH could be described as due to the protonation and deprotonation of the different oxygenated and amino functional groups on the surface of CDs [46,47]. The fluorescent intensity gradually increases from pH 4–6, while the fluorescent intensity decreases gradually as pH increases from 8 to 10. On the other hand, the GLP-CDs exhibit the strongest fluorescent emission intensity when the pH value is 7.0, which is similar to the physiological pH environment (pH = 7–8). The fluorescent response in the acidic range was stronger than that of the basic range and did not exhibit any color change, while increasing the pH from 8 to 10 can turn the color of the solution from yellowish to dark brown (Fig. 4b). The variations in pH can enforce protonation or deprotonation and shifting of the Fermi level of emissive sites in the GLP-CDs cores; therefore, the fluorescent intensity of the proposed CDs under alkaline pH conditions was significantly decreased [48,49].

The schematic representation of this mechanism is shown in Fig. 4c. Based on the obtained results, it can be concluded that the protonation and deprotonation phenomenon is responsible for the pH-responsive behavior of GLP-CDs. However, the exact fluorescent mechanisms of CDs are still contentious, and the same is true for pH-sensitive CDs, which could result from the complex and indefinite structures and the existence of different emissive sites [50].

The sensing response of GLP-CDs fluorescent probes to different ammonia concentrations was also studied. Fig. 4d illustrates the sensitivity of GLP-CDs against the NH3 solution, where the concentration of NH3 linearly differed, and the corresponding fluorescent intensity at each concentration was recorded. The fluorescent intensity (at ∼440 nm) of the aqueous solution of GLP-CDs was gradually decreased due to fluorescent quenching regarding the concentration range of 1–100 µM, which confirms that the fluorescence performance of GLP-CDs was significant for the colorimetric sensing of NH3 in a wide concentration range. In addition, the calibration plot between quenching efficiency (F/F0) against varying concentrations of NH3 in Fig. 4e displays a good linear-line relationship. At this point, F and F0 display the fluorescent intensity of the GLP-CDs with and without NH3 at ∼440 nm. The linear regression equation can be represented as Eq. (1).
(1)
F
/
F
0
= − 0.00815 × c
μ M
+ 0.9076
R
2
= 0.993

Moreover, the corresponding limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) for the GLP-CDs were calculated to be 0.40 µM, and 1.31 µM, respectively (Eqs. S3 and S4, Supporting Information) as the calibration plot was recorded linear up to 100 µM. Therefore, the proposed linear dynamic range was from 1 to 100.0 µM. The above results suggested that the GLP-CDs were suitable for ammonia detection.

The GLP-CDs indicator's visible color shifts under phosphate buffer solutions of different pH are shown in Fig. 5a. A good immobilization of GLP-CDs onto the paper matrix without any chemical alteration is explained by the pH-induced color change of the GLP-CDs indicator being identical to the color seen in the GLP-CDs solution. The color was bright yellow at pH 4–6, and at pH 7, it became brownish. Up to pH 9, this hue became more intense. At pH 10, the deepest brown hue was seen. As a result, it is possible to employ the GLP-CDs indicator as a pH-based food freshness indicator.

Fig. 5b displays the GLP-CDs indicator's measured color components. At varying pH levels, the indicator's L* value drastically fell; it was 58.6 at pH 4 and 20.3 at pH 10. The a* value considerably increased as pH was raised, peaking at pH 10. The b* value significantly decreased from pH 4 to pH 10, reaching its lowest point at pH 10. The colorimetric measurements of both the GLP-CDs solution (Fig. 4a) and the GLP-CDs indicator (Fig. 5a) were found to be consistent. The clear color change shown in the GLP-CDs indicator is said to be better suitable for identifying food spoiling when compared in terms of detectability with the naked eye since the indicator's ΔE was more than 5 at alkaline pH [15].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity to acid and ammonia vapors
ce:section.ce:para: The GLP-CDs indicator with pH-sensitive color-changing capabilities may be a suitable choice to detect pH changes in meat products because volatile substances like ammonia and trimethylamine are typically formed during the decomposition of meat products [51]. The sensitivity to pH change as a gas sensor was tested by the color change of the GLP-CDs indicator to ammonia and acid vapors. The indicator's original yellowish-red color changed to a dark-brown hue in reaction to ammonia fumes, as seen in Fig. 5c. In contrast, it turned a pale-yellow tint when exposed to acetic acid vapor. Ammonia vapor seeped into the paper, hydrolyzed into hydroxyl ions, and raised the pH of the indicator, which caused the color shift. The L* , a* , and b* values and the total color difference (ΔE) of the GLP-CDs indicator were measured during the period of exposure to ammonia and acid for 30 min to quantify the colorimetric response. The indicator's rising a* and b* values further supported the hue shift towards dark brown. Notably, the GLP-CDs indicator produces ΔE values above 5, demonstrating that the color variations may be easily seen with the unaided human eye. These results demonstrated that the GLP indicator was quickly responsive to gaseous ammonia and sensitive to it. As volatile amines are released when seafood products degrade, the color indicator, which changes color visibly in reaction to ammonia vapor, can be used as a freshness indicator to track pH changes in packed seafood.

Color stability, which is directly impacted by the structural characteristics of the solid matrix, is a crucial component in determining the quality of intelligent indicators. Color values and ΔE variations were used to quantitatively determine the color changes of the indicator during storage [52]. Table S3 (Supporting Information) displays the color performance of a GLP-CDs indicator that was kept at room temperature for 60 days. When the color characteristics are considered, the GLP-CDs indicator had a low ΔE value over the storage period. The cellulose's porous nature may give rise to the theory that paper can prevent and lessen oxidation by enclosing CDs, minimizing the effects of light, oxygen, and their chemical constitution, and maximizing stabilities. The obtained results reveal that the fabricated GLP-CDs indicator is very stable as the color performance of the indicator is reliable without substantial change even after storage for 60 days at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of GLP-CDs indicator for shrimp packaging
ce:section.ce:para: Herein, we conducted a food application (shrimp) to evaluate the effectiveness of the GLP-CDs indicator. Shrimp spoilage by microorganisms and endogenous proteolysis produce volatile alkaline gases (such as volatile basic amines (TVB-N) during storage [53]. Therefore, shrimp tend to have higher pH values with longer storage periods, which can be detected using ammonia-sensitive indicators. In this regard, the proposed indicator was implanted on the inner side of the packaging box containing fresh shrimp without direct contact ( Fig. 6), and the color variation of the assembled packaging boxes kept at 28 °C for 24 h was monitored. Previous studies have discovered that microbial activity causes meat and seafood to deteriorate [54]. The seafood’s pH may alter due to this microbial contamination, forming large amounts of total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N). Dong et al. have demonstrated that as the TVB-N value rises to 25 mg/100 g, protein in shrimp begins to break down and create organic amines [55]. In this study, the shrimp's TVB-N value steadily exceeded the upper limit (25 mg/100 g), which is permissible for edible shrimp. In addition, the fresh shrimp had a pH of 6.5, as shown in Table S4 (Supporting Information), which increased to 7.7 after 24 h of storage. This is similar to earlier studies, which found that the pH of shrimp increased from 5.8 to 7.6 as storage duration increased [53]. The sensing images of the GLP-CDs indicator under daylight and UV light are displayed in Fig. 6. After 24 h, the indicator's color changed from yellow (the fresh stage) to brown (the spoiled stage). The transition to brown color was quantitatively confirmed when the b* value dropped from 20.3 to 14.6. The obtained color change could be due to the accumulation of the emitted TVB-N gas into the GLP-CDs indicator. After storage of 24 h, the GLP-CDs indicator underwent the color shift from yellow to brown (L* = 54.1, a* = 8.2, b* = 14.6, ΔE = 12) under daylight, along with the emission of green fluorescence under UV irradiation. This analysis verified that the GLP-CDs indicator was suitable for monitoring shrimp freshness. The GLP-CDs indicator's color change in reaction to rotten shrimp was categorically unique, and it can be seen even by inexperienced panelists, according to the large ΔE value [15]. Because the GLP-CDs indicator has a two-stage color change that reflects shrimp in consumable-spoiled states, it has the potential to identify shrimp spoilage.

To determine the freshness of shrimp, we also established a link between the TVB-N content and the ΔE value. With correlation coefficients of 0.92, the relationship between the shrimp TVB-N and the ΔE of the GLP-CDs indicator was feasible and strong. To our delight, the TVB-N analyses of the rotten shrimp correlated with GLP-CDs indication, which confirms the effectiveness of the proposed indicator for quick, non-contact, and visual determination of real-time freshness of the seafood.

Fluorescent CDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method using GLP. The prepared GLP-CDs were rich in oxidized surface functional groups capable of selectively reacting to changes in pH. The color of GLP-CDs changes from light yellow (pH 4) to dark brown (pH 8), which determines the pH-dependent colorimetric behavior of CDs. Based on the pH sensing capability of GLP-CDs, a filter paper-based colorimetric indicator has been developed using the easy-to-apply drop-coating deposition of GLP-CDs. This newly developed GLP-CDs indicator has proven satisfactory as a pH-sensitive detectable spoilage system. As a result of measuring the freshness of the shrimp using the GLP-CDs indicator, the indicator's color changed according to the TVB-N content and the change in the pH of the shrimp during storage. The clear visual indication of the GLP-CDs indicator can communicate food quality directly to consumers. Therefore, the pH-sensitive GLP-CDs indicator has a high potential to use for real-time monitoring of seafood quality and freshness throughout the supply chain.

Ajahar Khan: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Parya Ezati: Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Jong-Whan Rhim: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Jun Tae Kim: Writing – review & editing, Data curation. Rahim Molaei: Writing – review & editing, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean government (MSIT) (2022R1A2B5B02001422).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2023.131242.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Dong, Z. Ling, X. Zhang, X. Zhang, S. Ramaswamy, F. Xu, Smart colorimetric sensing films with high mechanical strength and hydrophobic properties for visual monitoring of shrimp and pork freshness, Sensors Actuators B Chem. 309 (2020) 127752. https://doi.org/https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2020.127752.
